critique_points.point_001: The paper lacks concrete comparisons to prior self-consistency decoding and unsupervised self-training methods, leaving the novelty and incremental benefits of LMSI unclear.
critique_points.point_002: There is no analysis or ablation of the majority vote threshold used in self-consistency filtering, nor reporting on data retention rates during pseudo-label selection.
critique_points.point_003: The rationale for mixing exactly four different prompt/output formats in fine-tuning is insufficiently justified, with no experiments exploring alternative format combinations or their effects.
critique_points.point_004: Fine-tuning comparisons lack fair supervised baselines using ground truth CoT paths or direct answers, and omit comparable few-shot prompting baselines matched for compute and data.
critique_points.point_005: Evaluation is restricted to reasoning-centric benchmarks, providing limited insight into LMSI's applicability to other NLP tasks where CoT reasoning is less natural.
critique_points.point_006: Reported metrics focus solely on accuracy, without detailed error analysis, uncertainty quantification, or confidence calibration of pseudo-labels.
critique_points.point_007: Key fine-tuning implementation details such as hyperparameters, optimizer, training duration, and hardware are missing, hindering reproducibility.
critique_points.point_008: Prompt templates and generation procedures for the mixed training formats are not fully documented, complicating exact replication of pseudo-label construction.

summary: This paper proposes Language Model Self-Improvement (LMSI), a method enabling large language models to enhance their reasoning capabilities from unlabeled question-only data by generating multiple chain-of-thought reasoning paths per question, selecting high-confidence answers via majority voting, and fine-tuning on filtered pseudo-labels in mixed prompt/output formats. Demonstrated primarily on a large PaLM-540B model, LMSI achieves substantial accuracy gains across several reasoning benchmarks and improves out-of-domain generalization, while also enabling self-generation of questions and few-shot prompts to reduce human input. Despite promising results, the work would benefit from more thorough comparisons to prior methods, deeper ablations, expanded evaluations beyond reasoning tasks, detailed methodological disclosures, and analyses of risks such as error propagation and computational efficiency.
strengths: Introduces a novel unsupervised self-training approach that leverages multiple CoT generations and self-consistency filtering to generate reliable pseudo-labels for fine-tuning.
Demonstrates significant performance improvements on multiple challenging reasoning benchmarks without any ground truth supervision, alongside enhanced out-of-domain generalization.
weaknesses: Insufficient empirical comparison to existing self-consistency and self-training methods, leaving the true novelty and advantage of LMSI ambiguous.

Lack of critical methodological details and ablation studies, including majority vote thresholding, training hyperparameters, prompt templates, and error analyses, limits reproducibility and interpretability.
questions: How does LMSI compare quantitatively to prior self-consistency decoding and self-training approaches when evaluated under matched conditions?
What is the impact of varying the majority vote threshold on pseudo-label quality and final model accuracy, and how much unlabeled data is minimally required for effective self-improvement?
title: Large Language Models Can Self-improve
